note_data.title: Radioactivity
keyPoints: Magnets have north and south poles - like poles repel, opposite poles attract
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, cobalt, nickel) are always attracted to magnets regardless of pole
Non-magnetic materials (glass, plastic) experience no force from magnets
Temporary magnets lose magnetism when magnetic field is removed, permanent magnets retain it
Electromagnets are temporary magnets made by placing soft iron in coiled wire with direct current
Magnetic field lines show direction (north to south) and strength (closer lines = stronger field)
Electric charge is measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
Conductors allow charge flow through free moving particles, insulators impede charge flow
Electrostatic charging occurs by friction when electrons transfer between rubbed insulators

definitions.definition: A material or object that produces a magnetic field
A region in which a magnetic pole experiences the force from another magnet or magnetic material
Measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
Materials which allow electric charge to flow through them because they have charged particles that are free to move
Materials which impede the flow of electric charge because they don't have charged particles that are free to move
Electric charge at rest on the surface of charged insulators

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of what happens, include key stages
State similarities AND differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: A radioactive source has an activity of 800 Bq. After 20 minutes, the activity has decreased to 200 Bq. Calculate the half-life of this source.
modelAnswer.answer: Initial activity = 800 Bq, Final activity = 200 Bq. 800 → 400 → 200 (two half-lives). Time for two half-lives = 20 minutes. Therefore, one half-life = 10 minutes.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Identifies that activity decreases by factor of 4

commonMistakes: Thinking magnetic materials are only attracted to specific poles - they're attracted to both poles
Confusing temporary and permanent magnets - temporary magnets lose magnetism when field is removed
Not understanding that only electrons move during electrostatic charging, not positive charges
Mixing up attraction and repulsion rules for both magnetic poles and electric charges
examinerTips: Remember magnetic field lines always point from north to south pole with arrows
When plotting magnetic fields, closer field lines indicate stronger magnetic field strength
formulas: Activity = number of decays per second
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Radioactive decay curve showing exponential decrease in activity/number of nuclei over time, with half-life intervals marked. Include axes labels and units.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to identify which safety precautions are needed when handling radioactive sources in school demonstrations
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q6 (5 marks) — students had to explain how radioactive decay can be used to determine the age of rocks containing uranium isotopes
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — candidates needed to identify the correct term for natural radioactivity from radon gas
This concept was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — students explained the random nature of radioactive decay and half-life calculations
This appeared in M/J 2022 P2 Q7 (6 marks) — candidates analyzed different types of radiation and their penetrating abilities through various materials

workedExample.question: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 8 hours. If a sample initially contains 240g of the isotope, calculate the mass remaining after 24 hours. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the number of half-lives that occur in 24 hours: 24 ÷ 8 = 3 half-lives
Step 2: After each half-life, the mass is halved. After 1st half-life (8 hours): 240g ÷ 2 = 120g
workedExample.answer: After 24 hours, 30g of the radioactive isotope remains.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for calculating number of half-lives (3), 1 mark for understanding halving process, 1 mark for correct working shown, 1 mark for final answer 30g

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused activity and count rate with the actual mass of radioactive material' — always distinguish between the number of decays per second and the physical amount of substance remaining

Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently forgot that radioactive decay is random and spontaneous' — always mention that individual atoms decay unpredictably, but large samples follow predictable patterns

Examiner Report 2022: 'Common error was stating that half-life changes with temperature or pressure' — always remember that half-life is constant and unaffected by external conditions for any given isotope
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a decay curve graph showing: exponential curve starting high on y-axis (activity/mass) decreasing smoothly toward zero but never reaching it; x-axis labeled 'time'; y-axis labeled 'activity' or 'mass of isotope'; clear marking of half-life intervals showing how activity/mass halves at regular time intervals; the curve should be smooth, not stepped, demonstrating continuous exponential decay.
note_data.quickRecap: Magnets have poles that follow attraction/repulsion rules, with magnetic materials always attracted regardless of pole. Magnetic fields can be visualized using plotting compasses or iron filings, with field lines showing direction and strength. Electric charge follows similar attraction/repulsion rules, with conductors allowing charge flow and insulators impeding it.
